In addition, the grid search method and three scenarios of optimization methods were applied to determine the best optimization method for the computational efficiency of the developed framework. The comparable factor of safety (maximum error of 1.86%) shown in this study compared to those obtained by commercial software demonstrated the feasibility of using ChatGPT-generated code for seepage-induced slope stability analysis. Furthermore, the use of optimization techniques enabled up to a 70% reduction in calculation time, and the relatively easy implementation of optimization methods using ChatGPT implies that the appropriate prompts in ChatGPT can provide a wide range of applications in slope stability analysis.
authors: Jaeho Kwak, Jongmuk Won
journal: KSCE Journal of Civil Engineering

title: Quantifying impacts of precipitation and evapotranspiration on future runoff in the Han River basin using the Budyko framework
abstract: • The contributions of precipitation and evapotranspiration on future runoff are quantified using the Budyko framework. • The aridity index (PET/P) is projected to decline, indicating a shift toward a more humid climate. • The elasticity coefficients for P and PET are projected to change insignificantly across future periods. • Precipitation contributes the change in runoff, contributing by 67%–84%, while PET contributes by 11%–24%. Changes in hydrometeorological variables due to climate change are expected to affect changes in runoff, posing challenges for water resource management and increasing the risk of economic and environmental hazards. This study simulated future runoff in the Han River basin using the GR4J model under different climate change scenarios. The climate elasticity approach based on the Budyko framework quantified the contributions of precipitation (P) and potential evapotranspiration (PET) to changes in runoff. Runoff was projected to increase for the near future (NF, 2021–2060) and far future (FF, 2061–2100) compared to the baseline period (BP, 1975–2020). Runoff increased by 22.39% for NF and 34.72% for FF under the SSP2-4.5 scenario and by 27.46% for NF and 48.45% for FF under the SSP5-8.5 scenario. The aridity index (PET/P) decreased by 0.08–0.09, indicating a shift toward a more humid climate. Because PET contributed 11%–24% and P contributed 67%–84%, P was identified as the main cause of the change in runoff. The findings of this study can be beneficial for water resource management and establishing climate change adaptation for the Han River basin.
authors: Da Hong, Jeongwoo Han, Hyun‐Han Kwon, Tae‐Woong Kim
journal: KSCE Journal of Civil Engineering

title: Optimisation design of geocell-reinforced high-speed railway embankments
abstract: • Investigating the settlement of high embankments under HSR train loads. • Applying an advanced simulation model by Plaxis API with Python remote scripting. • Developing a reliable settlement estimation using the GEP algorithm. • Optimising the design of the GRPS system with geocell reinforcement. The construction of high-speed railway (HSR) embankments on soft ground areas is a geotechnical challenge due to excessive settlement and long-term deformation. Thus, this study evaluates the effectiveness of deep cement mixing (DCM) piles reinforced by a geocell system (GPRS) as a ground improvement method. An advanced automated simulation framework using Python remote scripting was developed to examine the effects of train load, embankment height, soft soil thickness, and the number of geocell layers. Approximately 336 data points on the settlement of the HSR embankment was extracted from Plaxis analysis. The highest equivalent train load was 100 kN/m 2 and the settlement of the embankment fluctuated from 22 to 65 mm. A Gene Expression Programming (GEP) algorithm was then applied to develop a reliable regression among the key variables with high accuracy (R = 0.99) and low errors. The parametric study indicated that the geocell reinforcement could significantly improve the lateral resistances, mobilise the membrane effect within the fill material, and reduce vertical stress and cumulative settlement. The research findings provide critical insights for improving the design process, optimising GRPS reinforcement using geocell, and enhancing predictive capabilities for HSR embankment settlement under various ground conditions.
authors: Van‐Ngoc Pham, Erwin Oh
journal: KSCE Journal of Civil Engineering

title: Applications of the spectral analysis of surface waves method in civil engineering – A systematic review
abstract: • Systematic review of various applications of SASW in civil engineering. • Detailed analysis of 37 studies sourced from Web of Science and Scopus. • Five thematic groups of SASW utilization across civil engineering domains. • Discussed methodological adaptations within the SASW applications. • Challenges and opportunities for the future development of the technique. The spectral analysis of surface waves (SASW) method is a geophysical seismic testing technique, widely adopted in civil engineering. This systematic review examines the broad applications of SASW by analyzing various studies sourced from Web of Science and Scopus. The identification, screening, and assessment of the sourced articles, following the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic reviews and Meta-Analysis (PRISMA) statement yielded 37 studies for the analysis. The SASW applications are categorized into five thematic groups: geotechnical site characterization, foundation and structural analysis, seismic analysis and microzonation, pavement and railway infrastructure, and specialized applications. These applications demonstrate the adaptability and effectiveness of SASW in various civil engineering scenarios, which are crucial for sustainable infrastructure development and maintenance. This review provides a comprehensive overview of SASW's contributions and methodologies, aiming to help practitioners and researchers optimize geophysical methods for engineering challenges that are aligned with ongoing advancements in the field.
authors: Muhammad Ali Rehman, Norinah Abd Rahman, Sung-Ho Joh
journal: KSCE Journal of Civil Engineering

title: Assessing water surface profile simulations with bulk flow resistance and composite roughness methods in partially vegetated channels
abstract: This study investigates methods for applying bulk flow resistance and composite roughness coefficients to calculate water-surface profiles for partially vegetated channels. The feasibility of each method was evaluated based on reach-scale experiments by comparing water surface elevations simulated by a one-dimensional numerical model with high-accuracy water level measurements. Bulk flow resistance coefficients produced consistently reliable results for scenarios with relatively complex vegetation patch distributions and densely vegetated patches regardless of flow conditions. The composite roughness method, which estimates total flow resistance using a momentum-based model, demonstrated strong agreement with observed data (RMSE < 1 cm and R² > 0.9) under low-flow conditions with a single vegetation patch. For densely vegetated patches, adjusting the drag coefficient further improved the accuracy of simulated water-surface profiles. The findings provide practical and explicit guidance for hydraulic modelers by outlining clear procedures for estimating and applying flow resistance coefficients in river systems characterized by spatially heterogeneous patches of emergent vegetation.
authors: Jiwon Ryu, Inhyeok Bae, Laily Fadhilah Sabilal Haque, Eun-Kyung Jang, Un Ji
journal: KSCE Journal of Civil Engineering

title: Low frequency vibration mitigation performance of a phononic crystal-enhanced floating slab track
abstract: The low-frequency environmental vibrations induced by subway trains have attracted widespread attentions due to their detrimental effects on human health and precision instruments. Based on the theory of phononic crystals, a novel phononic crystal-enhanced floating slab track (PCFST) is proposed as an enhancement to the conventional steel spring floating slab track (SSFST), by introducing an elastic wave bandgap to elevate the low-frequency vibration mitigation performance. A periodic half-track model is established and subsequently solved by the spectral element method, elucidating the bandgap characteristics of the PCFST. Then, a coupled dynamic model of the vehicle-PCFST-foundation system is built to calculate the vibration responses of the track-foundation system under train loads, providing insights into the PCFST vibration mitigation performance. Furthermore, a laboratory hammer-impact test of the scaled PCFST model is conducted. The results indicate that the resonators in the PCFST contribute a bending wave bandgap of 10-15.28 Hz to the SSFST. The PCFST significantly improves the vibration mitigation performance at center frequencies within 8-16 Hz, and effectually addresses the vibration amplification issue associated with the low-frequency resonance of the floating slab. The accuracy of the simulation results and the effectiveness of the PCFST are corroborated by a scaled PCFST model test.
authors: Xi Sheng, Rui Tang, Xu Yanjun, Bo Yang, Hanlin Liu
journal: KSCE Journal of Civil Engineering

title: Study on the mechanical behavior of loess arch and soil arching effect area
abstract: The loess arch is the important bearing substructure for the loess cave dwelling. In order to study the mechanical behaviors of the loess arch, four models with different thickness of covering soil were tested under vertical static load. The failure patterns, bearing capacity, deformation, distribution of soil pressure and soil arching effect were analyzed. Based on the experimental study, the finite element model of loess arch was established. The calculation results and the test results agreed well, and the soil arching effect area of loess arch was discussed. The results showed that the loess arch experienced elastic stage, elastoplastic stage and failure stage during the loading process. The bearing capacity of loess arch increased as the increasing of thickness of covering soil. The ultimate vertical displacement of arch crown was larger than 10mm, far greater than the corresponding limit value of the loess cave dwelling. At the same height, the distribution of vertical soil pressure was V-shaped in the proportional limit point, except the arch foot plane. Above the arch crown, the closer to the arch crown, the more significant the soil arching effect was. Finally, the function of boundary line of the soil arching effect area was determined.
authors: Feng‐Liang Zhang, Zheng Su, Zuqiang Liu, Shuai Liu
journal: KSCE Journal of Civil Engineering

title: Uniaxial compression tests of different types of encased recycled concrete aggregate columns
abstract: Encased recycled concrete aggregate (RCA) columns can be employed in soft soil foundations for enhancing bearing capacity, minimizing settlement, and expediting consolidation. This study examines the impact of various encased materials (iron mesh, aluminum mesh, rhombus and ellipse polyethylene mesh), gravel particle sizes (2-6 mm and 6-10 mm), and pile length–to–diameter ratios (5.71, 3.19, and 1.96) on the mechanical and deformation characteristics of encased RCA columns, using uniaxial compression tests. The findings reveal that iron mesh encased RCA columns exhibit higher pile top stress, whereas polyethylene mesh encased columns demonstrate greater radial deformation. When the quantity of gravel is consistent, larger gravel particle sizes tend to elevate the pile top stress of the encased RCA columns, while smaller gravel particle sizes tend to increase the bulging deformation. Throughout the compression process, encased RCA columns with larger length–to–diameter ratios undergo a bending phenomenon, whereas columns with smaller length–to–diameter ratios manifest an increase in the bulging strain rate. The study examines the stress–strain response and particle breakage of various encased RCA columns under uniaxial compression. The results inform the selection of encasement materials, RCA particle size, and pile diameter ratio, providing a scientific basis for using RCA in granular columns foundations for soft soil improvement and promoting sustainable reuse of construction waste.
authors: Chengliang Ji, Zhongfa Xie, Guangfa Gao
journal: KSCE Journal of Civil Engineering

title: The Hualien Earthquake triggered seismic activity in South Korea
abstract: The Hualien Earthquake (HEQ) of April 3, 2024, with a magnitude of 7.2 (7.4 by USGS), had significant geophysical impacts not only in Taiwan but also in South Korea, located approximately 1700 km away from the epicenter. Following the HEQ and its large aftershocks (M > 6.0), gravity measurements in South Korea recorded three distinct disturbances, temporally aligned with the mainshock and subsequent seismic events. Simultaneously, micro-seismic activity intensified along the northeast–southwest trending intraplate tectonic boundary between the Okcheon Belt and Yeongnam Massif. These findings suggest a potential case of transient stress triggering , whereby seismic energy from a distant earthquake perturbs the regional stress field and reactivates intraplate fault systems. The sustained increase in seismicity over 40 days following the HEQ highlights the importance of integrating gravity monitoring with seismic observation systems to detect and interpret remote geophysical responses. This study provides new insight into the possibility that the Korean peninsula, typically considered a tectonically stable region, may be vulnerable to dynamic stress effects from distant large-magnitude earthquakes such as the HEQ.
authors: Sungchan Choi, Sung-Wook Kim, Eun-Kyeong Choi, Kyu‐Hwan Lee, Dongyeon Lee
journal: KSCE Journal of Civil Engineering

title: Sound and vibration control of steel-concrete composite beam bridges in high-speed railways using constrained damping layers
abstract: This study establishes a vehicle-rail-bridge coupled vibration simulation model based on vehicle-bridge coupling vibration theory to analyze the dynamic responses of the train and bridge. A sound-vibration prediction model for steel-concrete composite beam bridges is developed using Statistical Energy Analysis (SEA, which assumes methods such as subsystem energy balance, weak coupling, high-frequency and statisticality) and bridge structure sound radiation theory. The study investigates the noise reduction effects of constrained-layer damping, focusing on the web plate, which contributes the most to structural noise. Results show that the contribution to noise from the subsystems follows the order: web plate, transverse diaphragm, top plate, bottom plate, and flange plate. The application of constrained damping significantly reduces structural noise across the entire frequency range, with a maximum field noise reduction of 5.5 dB(A). The effectiveness of vibration and noise reduction Nimproves with increasing damping layer and constraint layer thickness, but a nonlinear relationship with an efficiency threshold is observed. The optimal damping thickness is found to be 4 mm for the bridge model considered in this study.
authors: Siyu Lin, Jie Shi, Yong Li, Yusen Lin
journal: KSCE Journal of Civil Engineering

title: Investigation of verticality criteria on aboveground oil storage tanks
abstract: Ensuring the structural integrity of aboveground storage tanks is crucial in the petroleum and chemical industries, where failures can result in severe human, environmental, and economic consequences. Verticality, a key inspection parameter, plays a significant role in tank stability, as excessive deviations can increase stress concentrations and heighten the risk of structural failure. However, limited research has examined the impact of verticality deviations on structural responses under both operational conditions (filled with liquid) and non-operational conditions (empty). To address this gap, this study investigates the effects of verticality deviations on structural performance across varying liquid levels. First, a comparative review of international standards is conducted to assess differences in verticality evaluation criteria. Second, field measurement data are analyzed to classify storage tanks into three deformation patterns based on top shell plate deviations. Third, numerical models incorporating these deformation patterns are developed to evaluate stress distribution and deformation under different liquid levels and planar tilting conditions. The results indicate that increasing tilting severity and liquid levels amplify both stress and deformation, highlighting the importance of considering these factors in structural assessments. The findings offer valuable insights for optimizing inspection practices and enhancing safety while ensuring cost-effective maintenance strategies.
authors: Jang Ho Park, S. M. Han, C. Park, Taeyong Kim
journal: KSCE Journal of Civil Engineering

title: Investigation into the prediction of creep crack growth rate in P91 steel under multi-axial stress state
abstract: P91 steel components are often subjected to multi-axial stress states during service, which can lead to creep damage and crack propagation, thereby reducing their operational lifetime. By employing the Wen-Tu multi-axial creep ductility factor, the relationship between ductility and creep crack behavior in P91 steel is analyzed to determine the strain under multi-axial stress states. A creep damage model based on ductility exhaustion is established to evaluate damage accumulation. The GE-EPRI method is used to compute fracture mechanics parameters, and a power-law creep model incorporating a threshold stress is applied to characterize the multi-axial stress field. Through the definition of constraint parameters and the development of a predictive model, the creep crack growth rate is estimated. Experimental results indicate that, under identical conditions, the crack growth rate in notched specimens of P91 steel under triaxial stress is higher than that in smooth specimens. Compared with existing approaches, the model proposed in this paper demonstrates improved agreement between predicted creep crack growth behavior and experimental data, leading to significantly enhanced accuracy in predicting the service life of P91 steel under complex stress states. This methodology offers a more reliable basis for safety assessment and remaining life prediction of P91 steel components.
authors: Xisheng Yang, Chao Wang, Jia He, Xu Zhu, Zhongwei Wang
journal: KSCE Journal of Civil Engineering

title: Blast loads and testing methods for the blast resistant design and performance verification of industrial plant facilities: A state-of-the-art review
abstract: The increasing risk of vapor cloud explosions in industrial plants, particularly those handling hydrocarbons, necessitates a rigorous blast-resistant design and performance verification of structural components. Vapor clouds and gas explosions generate shock and pressure waves; however, current blast-resistant design guidelines focus primarily on shock waves. This study aims to enhance blast-resistant design guides and performance evaluation techniques by analyzing the characteristics of blast waves and reviewing the applicability of current blast-testing methods. The lack of research on the structural performance under gas and vapor cloud explosions underscores the need for further investigation. To address this gap, this study examines the potential improvements and applications of various blast-testing methods. Key factors, such as the types of blast waves, specimen types, current testing capabilities, and relevant standards, were compared. This paper also proposes essential improvements to advance blast-testing methodologies for industrial facilities.
authors: Seung-Hoon Lee, Suhyun Kim, Gwang-Je Choe, Han‐Soo Kim
journal: KSCE Journal of Civil Engineering

title: Optimization study on the parameter design of double-layer steel truss bridges based on the response surface method
abstract: To optimize the design parameters of double-layer steel truss bridges, ensuring structural safety while minimizing material usage. A combination of the finite element method (FEM) and the response surface method (RSM) is employed to perform rational parameter optimization. From the analysis results and the mechanical characteristics of the structure, four key influencing factors-elastic modulus of steel, deck thickness, truss height, and side-to-midspan ratio were selected as optimization parameters. The objective functions considered included the midspan deflection of the main and side spans, maximum axial forces in the upper and lower chords and diagonal members, as well as material usage in the deck and diagonal members. The AdaDelta optimization method was then applied to optimize the design parameters, and the results were validated using the multi-objective genetic algorithm NSGA-II. After optimization, the midspan deflection of the main span decreased by 24.81%, while the side span deflection increased by 20.53% but remained lower than that of the main span. Additionally, the maximum axial force in the lower chords was reduced by 24.6%, and the maximum axial force in the diagonal members dropped by 15.34%. Furthermore, material usage in the deck and diagonal members was reduced by 2.92%.
authors: Xudong Ren, Rongjie Xi, Zuo Kang, Lingbo Wang, Lei Wang
journal: KSCE Journal of Civil Engineering

title: Workability of Backfill Materials for Tail Void Treatment in Shield Tunneling: A Comprehensive Review
abstract: Tail void treatment using backfill grout is a critical component of shield tunneling using a tunnel boring machine to prevent ground subsidence and groundwater inflow. However, the lack of standardized performance-based criteria in the South Korea limits the evaluation of backfill grout, particularly its workability in a fresh state. Accordingly, we comprehensively reviewed the current technologies, material classifications, and field applications related to backfill grouts in tail-void filling. Backfill materials are categorized according to their constituents and injection systems, including single- and double-component methods. We investigated the key factors influencing workability—fluidity, viscosity, bleeding, and setting time (based on laboratory and field-case analyses)—and the influence of ground type and conditions on backfill grout mix design and performance characteristics. This study emphasizes that the selection of materials and mix proportions should be adapted to site-specific conditions for effective application. Double-component grouts with specialized additives are particularly suitable for challenging environments where rapid setting and high early strength are required. By integrating technical insights from both experimental research and field practice, this review provides a foundation for developing performance-based design standards and supporting the advancement of shield tunneling technologies in the South Korea.
authors: Jong‐Won Lee, Minji Kim, Hee-Hwan Ryu, Tae-Min Oh
journal: KSCE Journal of Civil Engineering

title: Mechanical behavior analysis of station construction by the tunnel-before-station pile method
abstract: In the current metro construction of urban core areas, shield tunneling frequently faces the challenge of passing through stations, especially when mined stations are incomplete, resulting in pronounced mutual interference between shield tunneling and station construction. The “group tunnel effect” commonly arises in urban underground construction employing the pile-tunnel method, intensifying the complex changes in surface settlement and structural stress. Based on the Memorial Hall Station of the Guangzhou Metro, this study employs model testing and numerical simulation to systematically explore how the “group tunnel effect” influences settlement deformation during pile-tunnel method station construction, and how shield tunneling through stations at various construction stages affects structural stress. The results indicate: (1) Ground settlement is primarily caused by pilot tunnel excavation and the installation of initial support with invert arches. The intersecting works of cross-passages with pilot tunnels and invert arches markedly intensify the structural interaction, and notable stress concentration occurs at the cross-passage’s initial support following the removal of the headwall.(2) If shield tunneling occurs before the invert arch construction is completed, it will lead to a significant increase in the segmental stress and a change in the stress distribution of the soil between the shield and the surrounding ground.
authors: Jimeng Feng, Jiacheng Song, Yifei Li, Xin Zhang, Junru Zhang
journal: KSCE Journal of Civil Engineering

title: Deteriorated hysteresis behavior of reinforced concrete multi-column bridge bents with varying axial loads
abstract: Multi-column reinforced concrete bridge bents are composed of more than two columns connected by a relatively rigid cap beam. Due to the framing action, the exterior columns would be subjected to increased or decreased varying axial load, and the total hysteresis behavior of the bridge bent is mixed. Two single-column specimens were tested under constant and varying axial loads to realize the effects of the varying axial load on the deteriorated hysteresis behavior of columns. An axial load variation equation was derived based on a two-column bent specimen tested in the literature to approximate the varying axial load. Test results indicate that the column subjected to the varying axial load would exhibit an asymmetrical hysteresis behavior in terms of the maximum strength, stiffness, and failure condition. The envelope curve of the column exhibits a negative and positive post-yielding stiffness under increased and decreased varying axial loadings, respectively. Compared to the constant axial load condition, the increased varying axial load would aggravate the stiffness degradation of columns by around 10% and slightly decrease the ultimate drift capacity by about 5%. Moreover, the increased and decreased varying axial loads would result in very different pinching behaviors upon reloading.
authors: Ping‐Hsiung Wang, Bo-Chuan Chen
journal: KSCE Journal of Civil Engineering

title: Investigation of the mechanical behavior of saline loess using unsaturated true triaxial tests and a constitutive framework
abstract: • A modified unsaturated true triaxial test to obtain detailed mechanical behavior of saline loess in the Xining Basin, located in the northeastern region of the Qinghai-Tibet Plateau in China. • The mechanisms of the effects of pore water salinity on the mechanical behavior of unsaturated saline loess are proposed. • An empirical constitutive framework (BBM-π) that considers osmotic suction is proposed, establishing three-dimensional loading-collapse (MOLC) surface and three-dimensional shear yield surface models. Unsaturated saline loess, characterized by significant dissolution collapsibility, is prevalent in arid and semi-arid regions (e.g., Northwest China) due to plateau and inland arid climates. The uneven distribution of soluble salts in these regions poses severe threats to pedestrian safety and infrastructure integrity, causing phenomena such as road heave and subgrade corrosion. We aim at exploring the impact exerted by pore fluid salinity (osmotic suction) and matric suction on the mechanical properties of unsaturated compacted saline loess under three-dimensional stress conditions. Saline loess from the Xining Basin in the northeastern Tibetan Plateau served as the subject of this research. An advanced suction-controlled true triaxial apparatus with rigid-flexible boundaries was utilized to perform isotropic compression and consolidated drained shear tests on loess samples with varying salinities under suction-controlled conditions. Mercury intrusion porosimetry (MIP) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) were employed to investigate the pore morphology and pore size distribution (PSD), analyzing the relationship between the change of the loess microstructure induced by salinity (osmotic suction) and its macroscopic mechanical properties. The findings indicate that increased salinity leads to the contraction of micro-pores and expansion of macro-pores in loess, thereby enhancing the compressibility and reducing the yield net mean stress. Post isotropic consolidation, macro-pores induced by elevated salinity are prone to collapse, which promotes particle compaction. Compared to non-saline loess, the effective contact area between particles in saline loess increases, enhancing its shear strength. Drawing from the Barcelona Basic Model (BBM), a semiempirical constitutive framework (BBM-π) that considers osmotic suction is proposed, establishing three-dimensional loading-collapse (MOLC) surface and three-dimensional shear yield surface models. As osmotic suction increases, the elliptical yield surface contracts longitudinally (along the long axis) and expands laterally (along the short axis).
authors: Shaoying Zhang, Shuai Shao, Shengjun Shao, Hao Wu, Zechi Wang
journal: KSCE Journal of Civil Engineering

title: Deep belief network for roof instability forecasting in TBM-excavated inclined shafts of weakly cemented strata
abstract: The collapse of roof strata in coal mine roadways significantly contributes to the instability of surrounding rock. Existing technologies for predicting the risk of roof collapse in inclined shafts constructed with Tunnel Boring Machines (TBM) mainly rely on statistical regression analysis or shallow machine learning methods. However, these approaches often inadequately capture nonlinear features and neglect the importance of temporal correlations. In this paper, we present a deep learning algorithm tailored for predicting roof collapse in coal mine roadways, focusing on the weakly cemented strata encountered during TBM excavation at the KeKeGai Coal Mine in YuLin, Shaanxi Province. We employ the Time Series Forecasting (TSF) algorithm based on Deep Belief Networks (DBN) to analyze a database of TBM construction parameters and past collapse events associated with these weakly cemented strata. Our study proposes specific evaluation indicators to assess the risk of roof collapse under such geological conditions. The results of our research indicate that when constructing inclined shafts through weakly cemented soft rock strata of the Cretaceous period in the western regions using the TBM method, four key indicators can effectively serve as early warning signs for potential roof collapse: VAF < 0.80, MRE > 0.10, R 1 < 0.85, and R 2 < 0.60. We also predicted the risk of roof collapse for twelve segments of strata at various locations in the main inclined shaft, successfully forecasting ten instances and achieving an accuracy rate of 83.3%. This validates the reliability of our proposed algorithm for predicting the risk of roof collapse.
authors: Hongwen Yao, M Li, Hua Cheng, Sun Xue-jun, Zhishu Yao
journal: KSCE Journal of Civil Engineering

title: Research on vibration influence range of double-shield TBM construction based on maximum Z vibration level
abstract: Double-shield TBMs have been widely employed in subway tunnel construction throughout China, particularly in cities with rock strata such as Qingdao, Chongqing et al. Whether construction vibrations affect residents' daily lives and how to determine their influence range have become pressing concerns. This paper first establishes the fundamental characteristics of double-shield TBM construction vibration through on-site vibration monitoring. Subsequently, the applicability of Z vibration level for evaluating these vibrations is investigated using a frequency weighting method. Finally, the vibration influence ranges in various types of urban areas are obtained based on maximum Z vibration level. The results demonstrate that the Z vibration level can effectively evaluate the impact of construction vibration on human comfort. In addition, the maximum Z vibration level generated by TBM construction ranges from 62.22 dB and 79.63 dB. The research results can provide reference for double-shield TBM design and construction, and it also can provide theoretical support for dealing with construction disputes.
authors: Zhigang Lu, Fulu Shang, Jian Cheng, Peng Zhang, Zhen Zhu
journal: KSCE Journal of Civil Engineering

title: Development of an ANN-based model for predicting unfrozen water content in sandy soils: Insights from experimental data and GUI implementation
abstract: Predicting the unfrozen water content curve is essential to understanding the thermal‒hydraulic‒mechanical behavior of frozen soil. However, existing methods often require extensive experiments. When experimental data is lacking, these methods rely on other empirical models, resulting in overly complex workflows. To overcome these issues, we developed two artificial neural network (ANN) models to predict the unfrozen water content of saline sandy soils for two distinct soil types. The trained model files were then implemented in a Graphic User Interface (GUI) program for end-users. The training datasets were collected through a series of laboratory experiments that varied void ratio, pore water salinity, and soil type using a newly designed freezing chamber system. A total of 186 datasets were collected and divided into training and testing sets. The training results have a coefficient of determination (R 2 ) greater than 99% for both ANN models. Meanwhile, the R 2 of test results were over 98% demonstrating the accuracy of predicting the unfrozen water content, closely matching the measured values. In addition, the trained ANN models are embedded in a GUI program for wide application in coastal and permafrost environments.
authors: Hyungmin Kim, Viet Dinh Le, Gyu-Hyun Go
journal: KSCE Journal of Civil Engineering

title: A multi-scale remote sensing for monitoring of heritage building through integration of satellite SAR, laser scanning, and drones
abstract: Due to advancements in digital mapping and remote sensing technology, monitoring building heritage has become more straightforward and convenient. Current individual measurements have numerous limitations, leading to their underutilization. This study suggests a multi-scale data monitoring approach for detecting building heritage deformation by integrating low-cost, wide-area satellite SAR data, high-precision laser scanning technology, and drone surveying technology for quick deployment and computation. Settlement monitoring of Sizergh Castle was conducted to assess the feasibility of the proposed method. The results indicate that the method outlined in this paper can effectively and precisely monitor the settlement and deformation of heritage building both indoors and outdoors. It can also identify the primary settlement and deformation areas within the building. This greatly improves the efficiency of monitoring heritage buildings and facilitates conservation and repair by maintenance staff.
authors: Lang Fu, Dongku Kim, Jun-Beom An, Chulhee Lee, Donggyou Kim
journal: KSCE Journal of Civil Engineering

title: The forward and inverse analysis of tunnel based on weak form physics-informed deep learning
abstract: In recent years, the advent of artificial intelligence based on artificial neural network promotes the development of data-driven methods. Nevertheless, data-driven approaches exhibit poor performance when data is limited. Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) have emerged as a compelling solution to address data scarcity. Although PINNs offer a breakthrough in handling limited data, it is rarely used in geotechnical engineering. To overcome limitations of traditional PINNs in geotechnical engineering, this paper proposes a weak form PINNs based on the principle of minimum potential energy. The proposed method constructs the force balance equation at the sample point through a weak form as the loss function of the neural network. Compared with traditional PINNs, the proposed method shows better training efficiency and robustness in geotechnical engineering. To validate the efficiency of this proposed approach, we take the diversion tunnels in the Jinping II Hydropower Station as a real-world engineering example. The example includes forward and inverse analysis of linear elastic problem and forward analysis of elastoplastic problems, and the results demonstrate that the proposed deep learning method has ability to predict displacement, stress fields around tunnels and the material parameters.
authors: Peng Yu, Ziyu Cui, Jing Liu‐Zeng, Yi Liu, Zheng Li
journal: KSCE Journal of Civil Engineering

title: Analysis of tunneling effects on existing adjacent tunnels using 2D physical model experiment and numerical simulations
abstract: As surface land in metropolitan areas becomes saturated, underground construction has become essential for expanding urban infrastructure, but excavating new tunnels near existing ones introduces challenges of stress redistribution, ground deformation, and potential instability, especially in diagonally aligned twin tunnels with asymmetric soil–structure interactions. This study presents an integrated framework combining physical model tests, Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV), and Discrete Element Method (DEM) simulations using PFC2D. Laboratory experiments with carbon rods replicated granular soil under six tunnel spacing scenarios, with excavation modeled by a scaled Tunnel Boring Machine (TBM), while ground deformation and segmental loads were monitored and used to calibrate numerical simulations, which were then extended to field scale with rock-like properties. Results demonstrate that tunnel spacing critically influences structural response: when an existing tunnel lay within the relaxation zone of new excavation, arching effects were suppressed and inverted segment loads decreased by up to 70%, whereas outside this zone, stress redistribution was more uniform, and load variations stayed within ±10%. A segmental load ratio–based classification system is proposed to assess excavation impacts, and the validated framework offers practical guidance for spacing, reinforcement, and risk mitigation, advancing safer and more sustainable subsurface development.
authors: Munkyeong Baek, Hee-Hwan Ryu, Ilhan Chang
journal: KSCE Journal of Civil Engineering

title: Failure mechanisms and stability of honeycomb-shaped tunnel group based on strength reduction: a case study of Hongyancun tunnel
abstract: Honeycomb-shaped tunnel groups lack sufficient design and construction experience. This study is based on the Hongyancun tunnel group project. A geotechnical similar material was selected based on the Mohr-Coulomb strength reduction theory. The material’s cohesion and internal friction angle can be quantitatively reduced through temperature adjustment. Indoor model tests were conducted to investigate the failure models and mechanisms of tunnel groups with various types of support. The conclusions are summarized as follows: (1) The physical and mechanical parameters of the geotechnical similar material prepared in this study can be effectively used to simulate the quantitative reduction of material strength in the strength reduction method. (2) For tunnel groups without lining, the safety factor was found to be less than 1. Initial failure occurred at the left-side intercalated rock, leading to extensive collapse. (3) For tunnel groups with primary lining, the safety factor was determined to be 1.2. Collapse was observed at the crown of the main tunnel, resulting in the progressive exposure of tie-back anchors, ultimately causing extensive collapse. Additionally, the local instability of a single tunnel does not lead to the overall instability of the tunnel group. (4) For tunnel groups with secondary lining, the safety factor was determined to exceed 2, and a crack line was detected at the tunnel entrance slope.
authors: Chao Kong, B. An, Junru Zhang, Jimeng Feng, Guang Qiu Huang
journal: KSCE Journal of Civil Engineering

title: Stress Relaxation Characteristics of Frozen Loess and Analysis of the Fractional Order Burgers Relaxation Model
abstract: To ensure the stability of artificial frozen walls, it is crucial to understand the long-term strength attenuation behavior of frozen soil. To this end, stress relaxation tests were conducted on loess samples from a western Chinese mine shaft under varying temperatures, moisture content, and strain conditions. Post-relaxation residual strength was also measured. Based on the analysis of the stress relaxation characteristics of frozen loess, a fractional-order Burgers relaxation model was developed. The results indicated that frozen loess underwent a three-stage relaxation process: rapid, decelerating, and stable relaxation. Initial stress, stress loss rate, and relaxation rate increased with moisture content and strain while decreasing with freezing temperature. The isochronous curves of the relaxation tests gradually shifted toward the strain axis, and the maximum attenuation ratio of the residual strength of frozen loess reached 29.82%. By performing parameter inversion, the parameters of both the integer-order and fractional-order models were obtained, and a unified form of the integer-order and fractional-order Burgers relaxation model was established. The fractional-order Burgers relaxation model exhibits higher fitting accuracy. The results revealed the stress relaxation characteristics of frozen loess, and the proposed model provides theoretical support for preventing engineering failures and enabling early disaster warning.
authors: Hang Wei, Zhaoming Yao, Xun Wang
journal: KSCE Journal of Civil Engineering

title: Using Fully Bayesian Approaches to Evaluate the Impact of Section Speed Enforcement System on Expressways
abstract: Effective speed enforcement is essential for reducing speeding-related crashes and improving road safety. Among enforcement methods, the Section Speed Enforcement System (SSES) addresses limitations of point-speed enforcement system by capturing both instantaneous and average travel speeds. Despite its recent installation on Korean expressways, previous studies of its effectiveness evaluation have limited. As such, this paper quantitatively assessed the safety effects of SSES using Empirical Bayes (EB) and Full Bayes (FB) methods with small sample data. The analysis covered four crash types, and the overall Crash Modification Factor (CMF) for total crashes was estimated at 0.653, indicating a 34.7% reduction in crash frequency. Additionally, heavy vehicle-related and adverse weather crashes showed safety improvements of 23.1% and 12.8%, respectively, after treatment. The Poisson-Gamma distribution provided the best model fit for crash frequency, while the Poisson-Lognormal distribution was more appropriate for crash severity. Moreover, downhill slopes were found to significantly influence crash risk, suggesting the importance of road geometry in speed management strategies. While this study incorporated spatial heterogeneity, further research including temporal heterogeneity and traffic flow analysis is recommended to enhance the reliability and applicability of safety evaluations for section speed enforcement systems.
authors: Songha Lee, Juneyoung Park
journal: KSCE Journal of Civil Engineering

title: Performance Evaluation for an Adjacent Metro Line Subjected to Ultra-Deep Excavation in Soft Clay: Case Study
abstract: • A 38m-deep excavation at a distance of 0.5m to existing tunnels was reported • Design strategy and construction implementation of the deep excavation were presented • Responses of adjacent metro line were illustrated by numerical study and field data This study presents a comprehensive investigation into deformation control strategies for a 38.3 m-deep metro station excavation in Tianjin's soft clay, situated 0.38 m from an operational metro line. Through integrated numerical modeling and field monitoring, the research elucidates dual-phase deformation mechanisms: The shallow excavation (0–20 m) induced 5.1 mm track heave from soil rebound, while the deeper excavation (20–38.3 m) caused 1.9 mm shield tunnel settlement due to consolidation effects. Horizontal stress redistribution concurrently generated 4.3 mm lateral station displacement toward the excavation. The transition from vertical unloading dominance to deep soil compression was mitigated using 1.4 m-thick diaphragm walls and sequenced top-down construction. Monitoring data demonstrate that coordinated management of time-dependent vertical rebound and progressive lateral deformation effectively contained displacements within safety thresholds. These findings validate a mechanistic approach for balancing ultra-deep excavation and adjacent infrastructure protection in soft soils, emphasizing the criticality of phase-dependent response control in spatially constrained urban environments.
authors: Zhong-Jie Zhang, Hang Wu, Peng Feng, Ming-Guang Li
journal: KSCE Journal of Civil Engineering

title: Gaussian Noise -Enhanced Prediction of Particle Breakage Strength in Rockfill
abstract: • Data Augmentation-Driven High-Precision Breakage Strength Prediction • Quantitative Dominance of Particle Size in Breakage Mechanisms • Mechanical Decoupling of Multiscale Shape Descriptors Particle breakage governs the strength and deformation behavior of rockfill materials, critically influencing the long-term mechanical performance and stability of large-scale geotechnical and civil infrastructures such as rockfill dams. To unravel the coupled effects of particle shape and size on breakage strength, this study proposes a machine learning framework enhanced by Gaussian noise-based data augmentation. The training dataset was expanded by injecting Gaussian noise with 1.6 times augmentation of the original feature values, simulating natural variability in particle morphology. Key input features—particle size and shape descriptors (e.g., Domokos shape factor for convexity and Yang roundness for edge curvature)—were selected through correlation analysis. Random Forest (RF) and XGBoost models were employed to predict single-particle compressive breakage strength. Results demonstrate that particle size dominates breakage strength, significantly outperforming shape factors (Domokos factor, Yang roundness). Data augmentation markedly improved model accuracy, achieving R² scores of 0.89 (RF) and 0.84 (XGBoost). This framework can be integrated into multi-scale simulation platforms for rockfill dams, enabling optimized zoning design through breakage strength distribution mapping and providing a novel tool for settlement risk early-warning.
authors: Zhenfeng Qiu, Ke Ding, Yi Tang, Wanli Guo, Ting Cao
journal: KSCE Journal of Civil Engineering

title: Autonomous Productivity Measurement NDT-SLAM-based Algorithm for Excavator Earthwork Operations
abstract: Earthwork operations are essential components of construction projects, with real-time monitoring of earthwork productivity being crucial for effective project control and resource management. Excavators play a central role in excavation and earthmoving activities. Although several advanced monitoring methods have been introduced, they require post-processing, limiting their ability to provide real-time productivity data.. This paper presents an NDT-SLAM-based algorithm designed for real-time productivity monitoring of excavator earthwork operations. The method generates real-time point clouds of the earthwork environment using a single RGB-D camera, allowing continuous tracking of excavation progress by recording the displacement of earthen material over time. It compares changes in volume over time to estimate productivity. To evaluate the proposed method's accuracy and efficiency, it was implemented in a test bed environment and compared with laser scanning as ground truth. The findings indicate that the proposed method reduced real-time data collection time by approximately 45%, while maintaining volume and productivity measurements within acceptable error margins for field applications. Furthermore, to validate its applicability in an earthwork environment, an implementation case study on an earthwork project is presented. The results indicate that the proposed method efficiently calculated the volume and estimated real-time productivity.
authors: Faten Benrouba, Abubakar Sharafat, AKM Ahsan Ullah, Jongwon Seo
journal: KSCE Journal of Civil Engineering

title: Performance optimization of microbial induced struvite mineralization: influencing factors, mineralization efficiency and sand fixation performance
abstract: This work proposes a novel soil ecological stabilization method using biostruvite, and optimizes the mineralization efficiency of struvite by varying the culture medium, ambient temperature and bacterial liquid dosage, and finally suggests the best proportion. The sand fixation performance of the biostruvite is tested to evaluate the cementation. Compared with a bacterial liquid cultured in Luria-Bertani medium, the bacterial liquid cultured in NH 4 -YE medium has higher urease activity and environmental tolerance and produces a significantly higher mineralization rate and yield. Increasing the bacterial liquid dosage and ambient temperature can both improve the struvite mineralization efficiency. The mineralization capacity of Bacillus Pasteurella cultured in NH 4 -YE medium is affected by ion concentration. In-situ sand fixation test results show that microbially induced struvite mineralization can effectively cement quartz sand while producing an uneven distribution of mineralized products in the consolidated sand column that decreases gradually from top to bottom. This characteristic makes microbially induced struvite mineralization very suitable for the in situ ecological solidification of soil by realizing gradient enhancement of soil mechanical properties from top to bottom, and improving the mechanical properties of the surface soil.
authors: Xiaofeng Chen, Hong Qian, Lijuan Xu, Yanping Wang, Xiao Zhang
journal: KSCE Journal of Civil Engineering

title: Modeling Intercity Travel Mode Choice: Uncovering Key Factor Interactions with Machine Learning
abstract: Intercity travel mode choice refers to the decision-making process individuals or groups undergo when selecting a mode of transportation, such as car, train, bus, for journeys between cities. This choice is influenced by various factors, including travel cost, time, convenience, comfort, environmental impact, and personal preferences. The key challenge in intercity travel mode choice lies in balancing diverse traveler preferences, such as cost, time, and convenience, with sustainable and efficient transportation solutions. Accurately modeling and predicting these choices is essential for optimizing infrastructure and policy decisions. This paper presents a Nonlinear Effects of Key Factors on Intercity Travel Mode Choice through the Application of machine learning for Enhanced Predictive Modeling and Insights. The proposed technique is the Viscoelastic Constitutive Artificial Neural Networks (VCANN). The main objective of the proposed method is to explore the effects of the influencing factors on the intercity travel mode competition and evaluate their dynamic effects across the travel distance. Initially data is collected from Travel Preference Dataset. The proposed VCANN machine learning model was developed and implemented using the MATLAB platform to predict the most suitable travel mode based on travel time, cost, and convenience. Performance of proposed method examined utilizing performance metrics likes Accuracy, Precision, Recall, Travel Time, Convenience and Travel cost are analysed. The proposed VCANN method is compared with the existing methods such as, Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost), Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM), and Geographically weighted random forest regression (GWRFR).
authors: S. Loganayagan, Murugesan Ramasamy
journal: KSCE Journal of Civil Engineering

title: Seismic Performance Assessment of Urban Transportation Networks under Complex Disaster Conditions
abstract: This study introduces a comprehensive framework for evaluating the seismic performance of urban transportation networks under complex disaster conditions, incorporating earthquakes and wildfires. By integrating seismic analysis with wildfire damage assessment and advanced traffic flow modeling, this research offers novel insights into the impacts of multi-faceted disaster scenarios on critical infrastructure. Utilizing the city of Pohang, South Korea as a case study, the research employs a SUMO-based traffic simulation to analyze the effects of disasters on the urban mobility framework. The paper quantitatively assesses the overall network performance through metrics such as total evacuation time and isolated vehicle counts, highlighting significant variations in network functionality under different disaster intensities. Key findings suggest that complex disasters impose a compounded detriment to transportation efficiency, markedly more severe than individual disaster events. The adopted approach not only enhances understanding of infrastructure vulnerabilities but also aids in the strategic planning of urban resilience and emergency response measures. This study’s methodology can be adapted for broader applications across various urban settings, providing a critical tool for disaster preparedness and mitigation strategies.
authors: Jooyoung Lee, Sungsik Yoon
journal: KSCE Journal of Civil Engineering

title: Research on the relationship between permeability and pore size distribution of soft soil in Zhuhai, China
abstract: Permeability is a crucial engineering property of soft soil that significantly influences its physical and mechanical characteristics. The permeability coefficient serves as the key mechanical parameter reflecting the soil's permeability. This study performed variable water head permeability tests and mercury intrusion porosimetry (MIP) on undisturbed soils from the Zhuhai area to obtain their permeability coefficient and pore size distribution. Among them, the pore size distribution obtained by MIP after the penetration test primarily exhibited a unimodal pattern centered around 1 μm. The effects of both buried depth and pore size distribution on the permeability characteristics of soft soils were investigated. The generalized capillary model, which incorporated the probability distribution of pore size, was utilized to assess the permeability coefficient of soft soil. This study also discussed the optimal pore interconnection parameter N p within the permeability coefficient prediction probability model and examined the influences of N p and minimum pore size on the predicted results. Finally, a correction method considering the influence of the inkbottle effect on permeability was proposed, aiming to enhance the accuracy of predicting the permeability coefficient of soft soil.
authors: Lizhen Feng, Dan Chang, Zhifeng Ren, He Zongjian, Kaijun Cui
journal: KSCE Journal of Civil Engineering

title: Wind Load Spectrum Modeling via Improved EMD Integrated with SHM Data
abstract: As the bridge span increases, the impact of wind load on the bridge becomes more significant due to the strong random characteristics of the wind field. Specifically, the wind speed is composed of mean wind speed and fluctuating wind speed, with fluctuating wind speed being the main component that causes the random characteristics of wind field strength. To address this issue, this study introduces an improved empirical mode decomposition (EMD) method to decompose wind speed which can automatically select decomposition layers. Additionally, the turbulence intensity, gust factor, and wind speed power spectrum are used as random characteristic parameters of fluctuating wind. Turbulence intensity and gust factor are calculated based on structural health monitoring (SHM) data. Furthermore, four wind speed power spectra (von Karman, Davenport, Simiu, and Harris) are established and compared. The results indicate that the von Karman spectrum has the highest correlation with the original wind speed spectrum based on daily and monthly monitoring data.
authors: Long Ran, Yang Ding, Da Hang
journal: KSCE Journal of Civil Engineering

title: Viscoelastic-plasticity Analysis of Surrounding Rock in Deep Buried High Geothermal Tunnels: Strength Parameter Degradation Approach
abstract: In viscoelastic-plastic surrounding rock, rheological processes gradually increase support reaction forces, affecting the stress state. Analyzing such rock requires consideration of stress path influences. In high-temperature environments, rock properties change with temperature, further impacting stress paths. Therefore, studying deep buried high geothermal tunnels necessitates analyzing viscoelastic-plastic surrounding rock while accounting for both high-temperature effects and stress paths. The coupling of high-temperature effects and stress paths influences the stress state of tunnel rock. By combining the Mohr-Coulomb strength criterion with the generalized Kelvin rheological model, we optimize stress, strain, and displacement calculations for supported viscoelastic-plastic surrounding rock. We derive analytical solutions for stresses, wall displacements, and plastic zone radii in viscoelastic-plastic surrounding rock, considering strength parameter deterioration due to high-temperature effects and stress paths. Using existing research on granite’s physical and mechanical parameter variations with temperature, we develop temperature-dependent fitting equations for granite's physical parameter deterioration. We theoretically calculate and analyze relationships among surrounding rock stress, tunnel wall displacement, and plastic zone radius. Results indicate that as temperature increases, its impact on rock’s physical properties significantly affects stress and strain. Moreover, as temperature gradually rises, the rock’s physical properties undergo additional changes, amplifying the impact of high-temperature effects.
authors: Jiahui Wang, Jiang Hai-bo, Lu Gongda, Jun Zhang
journal: KSCE Journal of Civil Engineering

title: Zone-Specific Prediction of Specific Charge in Tunnel Blasting with Machine Learning
abstract: Optimizing the specific charge in tunnel blasting is critical for excavation efficiency and stability. However, traditional approaches apply uniform charging across tunnel rounds, ignoring the distinct requirements of different blast zones. While previous studies have acknowledged these zone-specific needs, a comprehensive machine learning (ML) framework for predicting charge in each zone has been lacking. This study addresses that gap by developing a zone-specific framework to predict the optimal specific charge for the cut, stoping, lift, and contour zones. Using data from 208 tunnel blast rounds from 18 Korean sites representing diverse geological conditions, we evaluated various machine learning algorithms including linear models, support vector machines, neural networks, and tree-based ensembles. Our evaluation found that Random Forest was the optimal model for the cut zone (R² = 0.937), while XGBoost performed best for stoping (R² = 0.797), lift (R² = 0.925), and contour zones (R² = 0.927). Furthermore, SHAP analysis revealed each zone is governed by distinct parameters, with cut type dominating cut zone predictions, while spacing, round length, and rock type are most significant for stoping, lift, and contour zones, respectively. The proposed framework provides engineers with a practical, data-driven tool to improve fragmentation, reduce overbreak, and enhance both safety and cost-efficiency in tunnel excavation.
authors: Jung In Kwon, Ho Seong Lee, Tae Young Ko
journal: KSCE Journal of Civil Engineering

title: Experimental investigation on operating conditions for augmented reality utilizing indoor building element’s images as markers for construction and facility management
abstract: Augmented reality (AR) is evolving as an onsite visualization tool that can facilitate the expedited retrieval and enhanced comprehension of design information in field settings. However, traditional approaches, such as marker-based and markerless AR, either require additional effort for marker installation or face computational challenges in recognizing reference targets stemming from the self-similarity of indoor environments. By leveraging the benefits offered by both approaches, this study investigates the suitability of using existing building elements with known spatial coordinates in a virtual model (e.g., BIM) as reference targets for AR implementations. Experiments were conducted to assess the performance of this approach using six building elements with diverse geometries and dimensions as reference targets across several distinct operational scenarios, including a variety of distances and viewing angles. The experimental results indicated mean errors of 0.315 m and 4.801° in camera-pose estimation, suggesting that building elements can serve effectively as natural markers under optimal working conditions. These conditions include capturing images from the frontal perspective, and maintaining distances no greater than approximately twice the longest dimension of the target reference object. These findings imply that existing building elements in indoor environments have the potential to be used as reference targets for AR implementation.
authors: Bilawal Mahmood, Sang‐Uk Han, Seok Kim
journal: KSCE Journal of Civil Engineering

title: Correlation Evaluation on the Foundation Subsidence Mechanism of Densely Distributed High-Sensitivity Structures in Substations
abstract: While most subsidence research focuses on urban high-rise buildings, this study addresses the unique challenge of densely distributed substation structures with varying sizes and irregular layouts. Densely distributed structures refer to the spatial arrangement of multiple artificial constructions concentrated within a defined geographic area. Substations, which serve as hubs for power generation and distribution, encompass numerous such structures that are highly sensitive to surface subsidence. While previous research on subsidence has predominantly focused on urban high-rise buildings, limited attention has been given to the interdependencies and subsidence sensitivities within densely distributed substation structures. This gap poses challenges to the design, operation, and maintenance of these specialized facilities. To address this issue, this study proposes an intelligent analysis approach utilizing a backpropagation (BP) neural network to assess the correlation effects of subsidence among densely distributed structures. A deformation correlation model is developed, and its application is demonstrated through a case study of a specific substation project. The quantitative impacts of adjacent structures on the subsidence of the main control building are analyzed, while the underlying influence mechanisms are qualitatively examined via numerical simulations. The results indicate a strong agreement between measured and predicted subsidence values using the BP neural network. Among the adjacent structures, the subsidence contributions to the main control building are quantified as 42% from the main transformer protection and station power room, 31% from the integrated pump house, and 27% from the protection cubicle. Sensitivity analysis reveals that the main control room and protection cubicle are most affected by structural weight, whereas the integrated pump house, alarm transmission room, and main transformer protection and station power room are predominantly influenced by structural size.
authors: Qing Peng, Canlin Li, Hong Ke, Xionghui Huang, Longzhen Ren
journal: KSCE Journal of Civil Engineering

title: Efficient AI-aided segmentation of concrete CT scans using a predictive annotation interface
abstract: Concrete is characterized by a heterogeneous mesostructure consisting of aggregates, mortar, voids, and the interfacial transition zone (ITZ). X-ray Computer Tomography (CT) image processing can capture the 3D mesostructure of concrete, enabling its reconstruction for generating numerical models. A crucial step in this process is the segmentation of CT images. However, common segmentation approaches, such as thresholding, are not feasible for concrete due to the overlapping grayscale values of aggregates and mortar, which make it impossible to define a threshold for their separation. To address this challenge, segmentation software tools based on trained neural networks have proven to be accurate and robust, especially in biomedical CT imaging. In this contribution, we describe a segmentation workflow for concrete CT images using the neural network-based software SuRVoS2. This workflow effectively segments the concrete mesostructure of unaltered samples into three phases: cement, aggregates, and voids. The segmentation procedure begins with a shallow learning step to achieve an initial segmentation of the volume with minimal manual labor. In the second step, a U-Net Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) is trained using subvolumes. The final step involves predicting the entire volume using the trained CNN model and results in accurate segmentation with F1-scores higher than 97 %.
authors: Benjamin Werner, G. Rauchs, Petr Havlásek, Ján Sýkora, Salim Belouettar
journal: KSCE Journal of Civil Engineering

title: Prediction of ground-borne vibrations based on simplified pile-driving analysis: Application to vibratory pile driving
abstract: Empirical formulas and numerical simulations are commonly used to predict ground-borne vibrations from pile driving. However, these empirical formulas highly rely on field measurement data and cannot provide a solid analytical basis, and complex numerical modeling can take too much time to be practical for engineering purposes. Therefore, this study proposed one method enabling the efficient and rigorous prediction of ground-borne vibrations due to vibratory pile driving. Simplified pile-driving analysis was performed incorporating the interaction of the vibrator-pile-soil system to calculate the ground-radiated wave energy at the pile-soil interface, wherein the dynamic soil resistances on the pile shaft and tip were quantified in terms of equivalent soil parameters. Wave attenuation equations were utilized to forecast variations in vibration intensity with distance by considering the distinct characteristics of wave propagation and the wave energies. Finite element simulations and historical measurement data confirmed the accuracy of the proposed prediction method. Utilizing the simplified pile-driving analysis with notable efficiency, parametric studies were performed to conclude that centrifugal force amplitude, driving frequency, pile diameter, soil-pile friction coefficient, soil shear wave velocity, and soil friction angle can have a significant impact on the ground-borne vibrations generated.
authors: Shiguang WANG, Li Zhu
journal: KSCE Journal of Civil Engineering

title: Perceptions of the influence of BIM digital models in cost overrun management: A construction education perspective
abstract: • BIM is perceived as an effective tool for reducing cost overruns. • 4D planning is perceived as the most influential BIM Use for managing cost overruns. • Incorporating BIM in education enhances skills for managing cost overruns. • BIM significantly influences the mitigation of cost overruns caused by activity delays. • Integrative projects using BIM foster the development of cost overrun management skills. Cost overruns are a critical challenge in construction management, with severe implications for the economic stability of projects. The Building Information Modeling (BIM) methodology has demonstrated considerable potential to mitigate the factors contributing to cost variances, highlighting the importance of robust academic training to enable its effective implementation. Despite the growing integration of BIM into construction management education curricula, there remains a gap in the literature regarding students' perceptions of its effectiveness in key construction management areas, with few studies exploring how future professionals view BIM's role in addressing cost overruns. This study aims to fill this gap by examining undergraduate and graduate students' perceptions of BIM's potential to help reduce cost overruns in residential construction. Using a specifically designed questionnaire and analysing an integrative project, data were collected from 45 undergraduate and 30 graduate students. The results reveal a varied perception of BIM's influence on managing cost overruns, emphasising its effectiveness in the early stages and its limitations in external contexts. The findings contribute to reviewing and adapting university curricula to improve the integration of cost management knowledge and BIM training. This would improve future construction managers' professional competence and enhance BIM's effectiveness as a critical tool in reducing cost overruns.
authors: Kevin Torres, Karen Castañeda, Omar Sánchez, Carlos A. Peña, Karol Bobadilla
journal: KSCE Journal of Civil Engineering

title: Formation and failure mechanism of Tangjiashui landslide induced by expressway excavation in Hunan Province, China
abstract: Failure of cutting slopes in transportation engineering pose great threat to the construction and use of such infrastructure. Based on site investigation, deep displacement monitoring, and numerical simulation analysis, the formation and failure mechanism of Tangjiashui landslide in Lin-Wu motorway, Hunan Province, China, were analyzed, aiming at providing references for slopes with similar conditions. The results indicated that poor engineering geological characteristics of the sliding body, inadequate survey and unreasonable construction sequence of the initial reinforcement were main triggering factors. The slope body composed of ancient landslide deposits was prone to form a potential sliding surface. The excavation at the toe of the slope triggered the landslide. During the initial landslide treatment, the anti-slide piles were insufficiently embedded, and the toe counterweight fill and the soil behind the piles were excavated in a single operation, causing the slope sliding again. The numerical simulation results indicated that the recommended prevention and control measures of a combination of piles and cables could effectively prevent the landslide from sliding again. Thus, during construction in sections with similar geological conditions, special attention should be paid to the slope structure, the properties of the geo-technical materials, and the sequence of protective structure construction.
authors: Dacang Wang, Zhaohui Song, Yuan Chen, Dongdong Li, Hai‐Ning Liu
journal: KSCE Journal of Civil Engineering

title: Deep learning-based defect identification in hydraulic structures: A comprehensive review
abstract: • This review introduces different image acquisition methods in hydraulic structures, including manual and unmanned methods. It outlines the reasons why hydraulic structure images need enhancement and categorizes different underwater image enhancement methods based on deep learning. • This review provides a detailed overview of deep learning-based defect identification in hydraulic structures, including classification, detection, and segmentation. It summarizes the advantages of these models in hydro-structural application scenarios. • This work synthesizes recent advances from a comprehensive analysis of journal literature, while identifying critical challenges and future research directions for hydraulic structure defect identification. Hydraulic structures require advanced inspection methods to address aging-related defects for operational safety. The emergence of deep learning has revolutionized defect identification through enhanced computational efficiency and superior model performance in complex engineering scenarios. This systematic review evaluates deep learning-based approaches for hydraulic structure assessment, focusing on three critical components: (1) the transition from manual inspections to unmanned systems for defect image acquisition and large-scale dataset construction in complex hydraulic infrastructures; (2) deep learning-based image enhancement methodologies, specifically convolutional neural networks (CNNs), generative adversarial networks (GANs), and Transformer architectures to effectively mitigate critical underwater imaging challenges including blurring, low contrast, and chromatic distortion; and (3) the application of classification, object detection, and segmentation algorithms in hydraulic structure defect identification, highlighting the high efficiency of YOLO series algorithms in object detection and the high precision of U-Net, DeepLab, and Transformer models in defect segmentation. Ultimately, this review systematically analyzes current technical challenges and proposes actionable research directions to advance defect identification in hydraulic infrastructures.
authors: Ming Cai, Haoran Wang, Xiaoyu Lv, Zhaowei Liu, Yongcan Chen
journal: KSCE Journal of Civil Engineering

title: The susceptibility assessment of rainstorm disaster chains in the Tumen River Basin based on convolutional neural network and bayesian network
abstract: In recent years, extreme weather events and improper land use practices have intensified natural disasters, frequently resulting in disaster chains. This study investigates the flood-landslide-debris flow disaster chain (FLDDC) in the Tumen River Basin induced by heavy rainfall. For the first time, a convolutional neural network (CNN) is integrated with a Bayesian network (BN) to assess and map FLDDC susceptibility (FLDDCS), addressing the limitations of single-hazard studies. First, spatial predictions and susceptibility mapping for floods, landslides, and debris flows were conducted using a 3D-CNN model, which achieved AUC values of 0.98, 0.96, and 0.99, respectively. Subsequently, the Interpretative Structural Modeling (ISM) method was employed to map the interactions among flood, landslide, debris flow, and 19 associated factors into a topological structure. This structure was transformed into a BN to evaluate FLDDCS. The results indicate that areas classified as very high and high FLDDC susceptibility collectively account for approximately 1.5 % (approximately 3404 km²) of the study area, primarily concentrated along low-elevation river corridors. These regions are characterized by relatively flat terrain interspersed with deep valleys and sparse vegetation. By combining CNN and BN, this study provides an effective framework for FLDDCS evaluation. The resulting FLDDCS map facilitates the identification of potential risk zones, supporting land-use planning in high-susceptibility areas and enhancing the local disaster prevention and mitigation strategies.
authors: Zili Wang, Hechun Quan, Weihong Zhu, Ri Jin, Guangri Jin
journal: KSCE Journal of Civil Engineering

title: Experimental and numerical investigation on seismic performance of a novel self-centering socketed bridge pier
abstract: To mitigate damage to the plastic hinge region of prefabricated assembled bridge piers under seismic loading and enhance their self-centering capacity, a novel self-centering socketed bridge pier design was proposed. This design incorporates polyvinyl alcohol (PVA)-steel hybrid fiber reinforced cementitious composite (HyFRCC) to minimize local damage and non-bonded post-tensioned prestressing tendons to provide self-centering capability. The mechanical properties of HyFRCC with varying fiber ratios were tested to determine the optimal mix. The seismic performance of the new bridge pier was evaluated through static tests and finite element analysis. Results indicated that using PVA-Steel HyFRCC in self-centering socketed piers significantly reduced damage in the plastic hinge region, increased load-carrying capacity by 21.1%, and enhanced cumulative energy dissipation by 16.2%, compared to conventional self-centering socketed piers. Additionally, combining unbonded prestressing tendons with HyFRCC led to a 28.7% increase in peak lateral capacity, a 33.02% improvement in initial stiffness, a 35.45% increase in cumulative energy dissipation, and a 75.7% reduction in residual displacement, relative to conventional socketed piers without unbonded prestressing tendons.
authors: Jianping Han, Yong‐Sheng Xiao, Haoyu Xue, Jiying Shang, Shitao Cheng
journal: KSCE Journal of Civil Engineering

title: Optimized route selection for urban utility tunnels based on prioritization analysis
abstract: To ensure the safety of underground lifelines, utility tunnels have been constructed in new cities; however, recent development projects are mostly composed of small- and medium-sized developments rather than the large-scale developments seen in the past, which makes it difficult to construct utility tunnels. Even in urban areas, the law allows the construction of utility tunnels through a feasibility study; however, there are no cases of installation to date owing to the lack of methods and procedures. Therefore, in this study, we investigated a methodology for selecting the optimal route for a utility tunnel in an urban city area using feasibility and economic evaluations. In previous studies, the optimal route was selected based on feasibility evaluations; however, in the proposed methodology, the results of feasibility and economic analyses are comprehensively evaluated to increase the objectivity of the conditions for installing a utility tunnel, and a comprehensive priority is calculated for the entire section under analysis. Additionally, a step-by-step procedure was conducted by selecting the southeastern area of Seoul to determine the optimal route selection method. Procedures and application cases were used to rationally review the routes and sections targeted for utility tunnels when selecting optimal routes.
authors: Won-Joon Oh, Choong-Yeun Cho, Min-Jae Lee
journal: KSCE Journal of Civil Engineering

title: A structured review of SLAM generation in the AEC industry: Technical framework, site-specific challenges, and adaptive strategies
abstract: The construction industry has remained relatively stagnant in productivity due to limited automation adoption compared to other sectors. Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) technologies have been increasingly introduced to address the challenges of robot autonomy in dynamic, unstructured construction environments. However, existing reviews predominantly regard SLAM as a supporting component, without systematically examining the generation processes and site-specific challenges. This study conducts a structured review of SLAM generation in Architecture, Engineering, and Construction (AEC) robotics, encompassing sensor inputs, feature extraction, state estimation, backend optimization, and map construction. Bibliometric and content analyses identify key limitations arising from environmental degradation and task-environmental misalignment. Adaptive strategies, including sensor fusion, semantic enrichment, and dynamic map updating, are critically evaluated.
